commit_message: feat(bq): add vw_close_won + fix vw_lost_to_competition false positives

proposed_decision.summary: Schema views for deal outcome tracking via CRD-matched FinTrx employment history
proposed_decision.decision: Register two complementary Tableau views in schema-config.yaml for win/loss analysis: vw_lost_to_competition (closed-lost SQOs, fixed to exclude re-engagements and Savvy-current advisors) and vw_close_won (closed-won/churned advisors with prior firm provenance and tenure metrics). Both use FA_CRD__c-to-FinTrx matching to establish advisor migration patterns. Views are documented with key_filters, annotated_columns, source precedence, and data coverage gaps to establish the shape and limitations of this outcome taxonomy for reporting agents.
proposed_decision.rationale: The commit introduces a data model pattern for tracking advisor outcomes by joining Salesforce Account/Opportunity/Contact records to FinTrx regulatory history via CRD numbers. The dual-view design (lost_to_competition as closed-lost mirror, close_won as closed-won counterpart) establishes a symmetric outcome tracking convention. Documenting key_filters (re-engagement exclusions, Savvy-current exclusions, licensed-advisor-only) and prior_firm source precedence (Opportunity.Firm_Name__c preferred, FinTrx fallback) clarifies how ambiguous cases are resolved and sets expectations for data integrity boundaries. This pattern is load-bearing for outcome metrics and future win/loss analyses.

alternatives_considered.approach: Single unified outcome view with outcome_type column (won/lost/churn)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Splits distinct business logic (re-engagement exclusion for lost-to-competition; account-status filter for closed-won) into conditional logic, making the view harder to reason about and consume. Symmetric dual views keep concerns cleanly separated.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Queries would need outcome_type-aware filtering and re-engagement logic in every consumer, increasing coupling to view internals.

alternatives_considered.approach: Store outcome derivations in a fact table rather than a view
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: FinTrx data refreshes monthly and account history changes weekly; materializing would introduce staleness vs. living metrics like months_at_savvy. Views allow daily recalculation of tenure without job orchestration.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Churned advisors' tenure metrics would lag actual current date; re-engagement false positives would persist until next materialization run.

rejection_reason: Replaced by corrected version — original narrated the commit; corrected version states the dual-view + source-precedence conventions as rules.

commit_message: feat(advisor-map): hide churned advisors + full-viewport map height

proposed_decision.summary: Hide churned advisors from map display while preserving geocoded rows for re-engagement
proposed_decision.decision: Expose account_status and is_still_at_savvy boolean in vw_joined_advisor_location. Map display consumers filter WHERE is_still_at_savvy = TRUE; the geocoding cron intentionally does NOT filter by churn, preserving geocoded addresses for churned advisors in case they re-engage.
proposed_decision.rationale: The view needs to serve two different filtering strategies: the map UI should hide churned advisors for a clean presentation, but the geocoding background job must continue processing their addresses since churn may be temporary and re-engagement is possible. Exposing the churn flag as a derived boolean lets each consumer independently decide whether to filter, decoupling display logic from data-preservation logic.

alternatives_considered.approach: Filter churned advisors at the view layer itself, preventing geocoding cron from accessing them
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would require either duplicating the view or creating separate views for each use case, and would lose historical geocoding work if an advisor re-engages.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Re-engagement becomes harder to support; data loss risk.

alternatives_considered.approach: Keep the churn flag internal and handle all filtering in application code
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Geocoding cron is a background job that would need to bypass the display filter, making the filtering logic fragmented and harder to reason about.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Filter logic scattered across display layer and cron; re-engagement path unclear.

rejection_reason: Decision was reverted 7 minutes later in commit ad69a60 (the d4 draft). The churn filter never shipped. Feedback: hook drafter should detect same-day reverts of structural commits and either suppress the draft or mark it pre-rejected.

commit_message: Utility Claude calls in analyst-bot must bypass the analyst system prompt + MCP
diff_summary: Non-analytical Claude calls inside packages/analyst-bot (e.g. the planSections report planner, and any future classifier/helper calls that are text-in/text-out with no need for BigQuery) MUST use callClaudePlain from packages/analyst-bot/src/claude.ts instead of callClaude. callClaudePlain issues a bare anthropic.messages.create with no system prompt and no mcp_servers, so Claude doesn't inherit the analyst behaviors (schema-context lookup, execute_sql, [CHART]...[/CHART] output wrapping). callClaude remains the correct entry point for the main section-processing pipeline where SQL execution and charts are required.

proposed_record.summary: Utility Claude calls in analyst-bot must bypass the analyst system prompt + MCP
proposed_record.decision: Non-analytical Claude calls inside packages/analyst-bot (e.g. the planSections report planner, and any future classifier/helper calls that are text-in/text-out with no need for BigQuery) MUST use callClaudePlain from packages/analyst-bot/src/claude.ts instead of callClaude. callClaudePlain issues a bare anthropic.messages.create with no system prompt and no mcp_servers, so Claude doesn't inherit the analyst behaviors (schema-context lookup, execute_sql, [CHART]...[/CHART] output wrapping). callClaude remains the correct entry point for the main section-processing pipeline where SQL execution and charts are required.
proposed_record.rationale: On 2026-04-19, schedule e2f910fa (weekly SGM Coaching Review) failed with `[report-generator] Failed to parse section plan: Unexpected token 'C', "[CHART]"`. Root cause: planSections was calling callClaude, which injects the full analyst system prompt telling Claude to always run SQL and wrap data in [CHART]...[/CHART] blocks, plus attaches the schema-context MCP server. The planner prompt ("return a JSON array of sections") was therefore interpreted as an analytical request — GCP logs showed 5 execute_sql calls plus a response starting with "[CHART]\n{". Because JSON.parse saw a response starting with `[` (specifically `[CHART]`), the fallback regex at :239 was skipped and parsing died on the 'C'. The fix introduces callClaudePlain and rewires the planner; verified in prod at 2026-04-19 16:32 UTC when the rescheduled weekly report ran cleanly, produced a Google Doc, and DM'd the user.

alternatives_considered.approach: Keep callClaude and strip [CHART] blocks + extract JSON array with a tighter regex before parsing
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Treats the symptom, not the cause. Claude would still burn ~2 min running 5 full SQL pipelines during planning, wasting tokens and BigQuery bytes. The planner is supposed to be a cheap structural step.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Any future prompt leaking CHART/XLSX/EXPORT_SQL patterns — or a model upgrade changing how the system prompt is interpreted — would reintroduce parse failures.

alternatives_considered.approach: Weaken the analyst system prompt so [CHART] blocks become optional
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: The [CHART] requirement is load-bearing for the analyst bot's data responses. Making it optional would degrade the primary user-facing feature.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Scheduled reports and Slack answers would stop producing charts consistently.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use a separate Anthropic client with its own config for the planner
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Overkill. A single shared client with a lightweight helper function achieves the same isolation.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: n/a

proposed_record.revisit_conditions: If we add a new utility Claude call inside analyst-bot (e.g. title generation, section summarization, classification), reach for callClaudePlain by default. Revisit if the analyst system prompt is ever split into composable fragments — at that point a scoped system prompt may be cleaner than zero system prompt.

proposed_decision.rationale: Editor backup files (.bak) are ephemeral artifacts of local development workflows and should not be committed to version control. This commit removes pre-existing backup files and establishes a convention to prevent future accumulation of such files in the repository.

commit_message: Analyst-bot report planner uses a bare Claude call (no analyst system prompt, no MCP) separate from callClaude
diff_summary: The report planner in packages/analyst-bot/src/report-generator.ts (planSections) must not go through callClaude(). callClaude is the analyst pipeline entry point and attaches the analyst system prompt (which mandates a [CHART]...[/CHART] block on every data response) and the schema-context MCP server (tool access, execute_sql). The planner is a meta-planning task that asks Claude to return a JSON array of report sections — it must call a separate, bare wrapper (callClaudePlain) that invokes anthropic.messages.create with no system prompt and no mcp_servers. The defensive [CHART] stripping and tighter JSON array-extraction regex added in the parser are retained as belt-and-suspenders, but the structural fix is the separate call surface.

proposed_record.summary: Analyst-bot report planner uses a bare Claude call (no analyst system prompt, no MCP) separate from callClaude
proposed_record.decision: The report planner in packages/analyst-bot/src/report-generator.ts (planSections) must not go through callClaude(). callClaude is the analyst pipeline entry point and attaches the analyst system prompt (which mandates a [CHART]...[/CHART] block on every data response) and the schema-context MCP server (tool access, execute_sql). The planner is a meta-planning task that asks Claude to return a JSON array of report sections — it must call a separate, bare wrapper (callClaudePlain) that invokes anthropic.messages.create with no system prompt and no mcp_servers. The defensive [CHART] stripping and tighter JSON array-extraction regex added in the parser are retained as belt-and-suspenders, but the structural fix is the separate call surface.
proposed_record.rationale: The planner's user prompt asks for a JSON array of sections, but when routed through callClaude the analyst system prompt wins over the user prompt on formatting contracts: Claude called execute_sql per section (treating planning as analysis) and wrapped output in [CHART]...[/CHART] blocks, breaking JSON.parse with "Unexpected token 'C', \"[CHART]\"". Surfaced in prod on 2026-04-19 when the weekly SGM Coaching Review scheduled report (e2f910fa-374d-47a3-8517-fd54ab08e155) failed. Fixed in commit 24c18f6. Prompt engineering alone ("do not emit [CHART]") is unreliable because system prompts override user prompts on formatting. Conditionalizing the system prompt inside callClaude was rejected — it would turn the analyst entry point into a polymorphic mess and erode clear LLM-call boundaries. Forking a separate callClaudePlain is the correct boundary: analyst calls (agent with tools + chart contract) and planner calls (plain JSON generation) are fundamentally different tasks and should have different call surfaces.

alternatives_considered.approach: Conditionalize the system prompt inside callClaude (e.g., a 'mode' parameter that suppresses the analyst prompt and MCP for planner calls)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would turn the analyst pipeline entry point into a polymorphic mess; callClaude exists for analyst reasons (tool access, MCP schema context, chart rendering contract) and muddying it erodes the LLM-call boundary

alternatives_considered.approach: Regex-strip [CHART]...[/CHART] blocks from planner output and tighten the JSON array-extraction regex
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Does not address the root cause — the planner was running execute_sql per section and behaving as an analyst rather than a planner. Kept as defense-in-depth but not the structural fix.

alternatives_considered.approach: Prompt-engineer the planner's user prompt to instruct Claude not to emit [CHART] blocks or call tools
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Unreliable: when system prompt and user prompt conflict on formatting contracts, the system prompt wins. The analyst system prompt mandates [CHART] on every data response, so no user-prompt instruction can reliably suppress it.

proposed_record.revisit_conditions: Revisit if: (a) the Anthropic API gains a reliable way to scope a system prompt to a single call without forking the client wrapper, making callClaudePlain redundant; (b) a third Claude-calling surface with planner-like needs appears, in which case factor a small "plain" vs "agent" taxonomy rather than keep two ad-hoc named functions; (c) callClaude's system prompt is broken into composable sections that can be opted into à la carte — then the planner could use callClaude with a null/empty section set instead of a separate function.

commit_message: Outcome-tracking views in schema-config.yaml use a symmetric dual-view pattern with documented key_filters and explicit prior_firm source precedence
diff_summary: When registering outcome-tracking views in .claude/schema-config.yaml (e.g., win/loss, churn, retention), follow two conventions: (1) symmetric dual-view pattern — one view per outcome state rather than a unified view with an outcome_type column, so each view's exclusion logic stays local and consumers do not carry outcome-type branching; (2) when joining Salesforce Opportunity/Account/Contact data to FinTrx regulatory history to establish advisor migration patterns, match on FA_CRD__c and document the source precedence for overlapping fields — Opportunity.Firm_Name__c is the preferred prior_firm source, FinTrx employment history is the fallback when Opportunity data is missing. Every outcome view must document its key_filters (re-engagement exclusions, Savvy-current exclusions, licensed-advisor-only gates, etc.), annotated_columns, and data coverage gaps so downstream reporting agents understand the taxonomy boundaries.

proposed_record.summary: Outcome-tracking views in schema-config.yaml use a symmetric dual-view pattern with documented key_filters and explicit prior_firm source precedence
proposed_record.decision: When registering outcome-tracking views in .claude/schema-config.yaml (e.g., win/loss, churn, retention), follow two conventions: (1) symmetric dual-view pattern — one view per outcome state rather than a unified view with an outcome_type column, so each view's exclusion logic stays local and consumers do not carry outcome-type branching; (2) when joining Salesforce Opportunity/Account/Contact data to FinTrx regulatory history to establish advisor migration patterns, match on FA_CRD__c and document the source precedence for overlapping fields — Opportunity.Firm_Name__c is the preferred prior_firm source, FinTrx employment history is the fallback when Opportunity data is missing. Every outcome view must document its key_filters (re-engagement exclusions, Savvy-current exclusions, licensed-advisor-only gates, etc.), annotated_columns, and data coverage gaps so downstream reporting agents understand the taxonomy boundaries.
proposed_record.rationale: The commit introduces a data model pattern for tracking advisor outcomes by joining Salesforce Account/Opportunity/Contact records to FinTrx regulatory history via CRD numbers. The dual-view design (lost_to_competition as closed-lost mirror, close_won as closed-won counterpart) establishes a symmetric outcome tracking convention. Documenting key_filters (re-engagement exclusions, Savvy-current exclusions, licensed-advisor-only) and prior_firm source precedence (Opportunity.Firm_Name__c preferred, FinTrx fallback) clarifies how ambiguous cases are resolved and sets expectations for data integrity boundaries. This pattern is load-bearing for outcome metrics and future win/loss analyses.

alternatives_considered.approach: Single unified outcome view with outcome_type column (won/lost/churn)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Splits distinct business logic (re-engagement exclusion for lost-to-competition; account-status filter for closed-won) into conditional logic, making the view harder to reason about and consume. Symmetric dual views keep concerns cleanly separated.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Queries would need outcome_type-aware filtering and re-engagement logic in every consumer, increasing coupling to view internals.

alternatives_considered.approach: Store outcome derivations in a fact table rather than a view
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: FinTrx data refreshes monthly and account history changes weekly; materializing would introduce staleness vs. living metrics like months_at_savvy. Views allow daily recalculation of tenure without job orchestration.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Churned advisors' tenure metrics would lag actual current date; re-engagement false positives would persist until next materialization run.

proposed_record.revisit_conditions: Revisit if: (a) BigQuery gains a native outcome-type materialization that makes dual views redundant; (b) FinTrx data freshness improves such that materializing outcomes as a fact table beats view recalculation; (c) Salesforce field precedence changes — e.g., Firm_Name__c deprecated in favor of a new standard field.

commit_message: feat(attribution): route SGA-filtered queries through vw_lead_primary_sga behind ATTRIBUTION_MODEL flag
feat(attribution): route SGA-filtered queries through vw_lead_primary_sga behind ATTRIBUTION_MODEL flag

proposed_record.summary: Route SGA-filtered attribution queries to primary_sga via ATTRIBUTION_MODEL flag
proposed_record.decision: When computing per-SGA conversion metrics (Contacted→MQL), route queries through vw_lead_primary_sga instead of current-owner SGA to eliminate sweep bias. Expose this correction as ATTRIBUTION_MODEL=v2 opt-in flag; v1 (current-owner, buggy) remains default and byte-identical for unfiltered queries. Surface AttributionDebugPanel in dashboard to debug and validate the correction.
proposed_record.rationale: Current-owner SGA denominator inflates per-SGA rates 3-15x when leads sweep to Savvy Operations, dropping them from the active owner pool. Using lead-era primary SGA eliminates this denominator distortion (e.g., Russell Armitage Q3 2025 correction from 61.3% to 4.4%). Backward-compatible flag and schema-config.yaml guidance ensure MCP server steers future queries to the unbiased model without breaking existing dashboards.

alternatives_considered.approach: Always route all SGA queries to primary_sga without flag
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would break existing reports and dashboards relying on current-owner semantics; no transition path.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Silent correctness at cost of backward incompatibility and customer confusion.

alternatives_considered.approach: Fix the denominator calculation post-hoc in application layer
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Defers the correction away from the source of truth; harder to maintain consistency across all query patterns and future MCP-driven queries.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Ongoing risk of queries being issued against stale schema without the correction applied.

alternatives_considered.approach: Deprecate v1 immediately
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: No grace period for stakeholders to validate v2 behavior; blocks decision on whether the 3-15x correction is actually correct for all use cases.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Unvalidated breaking change rolled into production reports.

commit_message: feat(attribution): route SGA-filtered queries through vw_lead_primary_sga behind ATTRIBUTION_MODEL flag

proposed_record.summary: Introduce lead-era primary SGA view to correct Savvy Ops sweep bias in per-SGA conversion metrics
proposed_record.decision: Implement vw_lead_primary_sga + ref_non_sga_users reference table to attribute leads to the SGA who owned them during the lead/contacting phase (immune to Savvy Operations reassignment ~90 days post-MQL). Route per-SGA filtered queries through primary_sga_name instead of current SGA_Owner_Name__c when ATTRIBUTION_MODEL=v2. This prevents 3-15× inflation of per-SGA Contacted→MQL rates caused by swept leads falling out of the denominator. v1 (current-owner) remains default and byte-identical for unfiltered queries.
proposed_record.rationale: Commit fixes a documented measurement bias: per-SGA conversion rates were inflating dramatically (e.g. Russell Armitage Q3 2025 dropped from 61.3% to 4.4%) because leads swept to Savvy Operations by automation left the current-owner denominator. The new view reconstructs lead-era ownership from LeadHistory, making attribution immune to the sweep mechanism. The opt-in flag (ATTRIBUTION_MODEL=v2) preserves backward compatibility while guiding the schema for future queries.

alternatives_considered.approach: Suppress Savvy Operations rows from per-SGA rate calculations without a join
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would break per-SGA rates in the opposite direction — leaves legitimate SGA denominator incomplete for leads they did work before sweep. Requires changes at query layer.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Understates true performance of SGAs whose leads swept mid-period.

alternatives_considered.approach: Materialize vw_lead_primary_sga as a daily table instead of computing per-query
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Added operational overhead (scheduler, staleness risk) not justified by current query volume. Re-evaluation per query is sufficient given LeadHistory sync cadence (6h).
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Materialized stale data conflicts with real-time lead reassignments during low-volume periods.

commit_message: For per-SGA conversion metrics on lead-era stages, use vw_lead_primary_sga.primary_sga_name — never v.SGA_Owner_Name__c alone
diff_summary: Any query that filters by a specific SGA for Contacted / MQL / SQL conversion rates MUST LEFT JOIN Tableau_Views.vw_lead_primary_sga on lead_id = Full_prospect_id__c and filter on primary_sga_name. Do not filter on v.SGA_Owner_Name__c alone. The helper buildSgaFilterClause in src/lib/utils/filter-helpers.ts is the canonical path and respects the ATTRIBUTION_MODEL env flag.

proposed_record.summary: For per-SGA conversion metrics on lead-era stages, use vw_lead_primary_sga.primary_sga_name — never v.SGA_Owner_Name__c alone
proposed_record.decision: Any query that filters by a specific SGA for Contacted / MQL / SQL conversion rates MUST LEFT JOIN Tableau_Views.vw_lead_primary_sga on lead_id = Full_prospect_id__c and filter on primary_sga_name. Do not filter on v.SGA_Owner_Name__c alone. The helper buildSgaFilterClause in src/lib/utils/filter-helpers.ts is the canonical path and respects the ATTRIBUTION_MODEL env flag.
proposed_record.rationale:

Observed distortion 3-15x — Russell Armitage Q3 2025 Contacted→MQL read 61.3% via current-owner vs 4.4% lead-era; Craig Suchodolski Q2 2025 read 25.8% vs 3.1%. The lead-era view is reconstructed from LeadHistory, excludes Savvy Ops / Savvy Marketing via ref_non_sga_users, and was validated Phase 2.6 against Gate D (Lauren George 9/272 = 3.309% exact).</rationale>
<parameter name="revisit_conditions">When Phase 4 ships the vw_opp_primary_sga companion for opp-era metrics (SQO / Joined / AUM by SGA). At that point this rule expands to cover both views.

alternatives_considered.approach: Keep filtering on v.SGA_Owner_Name__c

alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Dashboard shipped with this pre-Phase 3; distortion validated against production queries (Russell +61% vs 4.4% lead-era)

alternatives_considered.approach: COALESCE(p.primary_sga_name, v.SGA_Owner_Name__c) on the filter predicate
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Reintroduces Savvy-Ops-sweep noise on opp-only rows — fallback captures the same data we're trying to exclude
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Discussed as Q5 council question on 2026-04-21; Russell rejected — see companion decision phase3-attr-20260421-noco

commit_message: Opp-era metrics filtered by SGA get a dedicated view in Phase 4 — do not COALESCE lead-era + current-owner in the WHERE predicate
diff_summary: buildSgaFilterClause's v2 WHERE remains strict `p.primary_sga_name IN UNNEST(@sgas)`. When Phase 4 adds opp-era attribution (SQO / Joined / AUM by SGA), it gets its own view (tentative: vw_opp_primary_sga) and its own predicate. Do not introduce a COALESCE fallback on the filter predicate under any circumstance. proposed_record.summary: Opp-era metrics filtered by SGA get a dedicated view in Phase 4 — do not COALESCE lead-era + current-owner in the WHERE predicate
proposed_record.decision: buildSgaFilterClause's v2 WHERE remains strict `p.primary_sga_name IN UNNEST(@sgas)`. When Phase 4 adds opp-era attribution (SQO / Joined / AUM by SGA), it gets its own view (tentative: vw_opp_primary_sga) and its own predicate. Do not introduce a COALESCE fallback on the filter predicate under any circumstance. commit_message: Feature-flag query helpers must short-circuit to byte-identical SQL when the filter is not active
diff_summary: Query-layer helpers that branch on a feature flag (e.g. buildSgaFilterClause on ATTRIBUTION_MODEL) must short-circuit to empty joinClause + empty whereClause when the filter is not active. This makes the emitted SQL byte-identical between modes in the unfiltered case, preserving aggregate invariance as a structural guarantee rather than a validation artifact.

proposed_record.summary: Feature-flag query helpers must short-circuit to byte-identical SQL when the filter is not active
proposed_record.decision: Query-layer helpers that branch on a feature flag (e.g. buildSgaFilterClause on ATTRIBUTION_MODEL) must short-circuit to empty joinClause + empty whereClause when the filter is not active. This makes the emitted SQL byte-identical between modes in the unfiltered case, preserving aggregate invariance as a structural guarantee rather than a validation artifact.
proposed_record.rationale:
Phase 3 Gate 6 requires 6 unfiltered baseline metrics to stay identical between v1 and v2. Rather than validate each query separately, the helper returns {joinClause:'', whereClause:'', params:{}} when sgasFilter is undefined or selectAll — any query interpolating ${clause.joinClause} produces zero-char output, so the compiled SQL text is literally the same regardless of mode. Structural invariance is more robust than test-based invariance because it holds under any cohort or filter combination, not just the ones covered by gates.</rationale>
<parameter name="revisit_conditions">When any future feature flag gates query-shape changes (e.g. Phase 4 opp-era ATTRIBUTION_MODEL branching, or any other v1/v2 migration on query helpers).

alternatives_considered.approach: Helper always emits a JOIN that filters to TRUE when flag=unfiltered
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Adds query-planner cost (unnecessary JOIN); breaks byte-identity property; forces validation-based rather than structural invariance
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Not tried — rejected at design time during Phase 3 guide review

commit_message: Flipping an env flag that changes runtime query results does NOT invalidate unstable_cache — must call revalidateTag after the flip
diff_summary: When a feature flag is flipped via env var to change the runtime output of a function wrapped in cachedQuery / unstable_cache, the cache key (derived from function name + serialized args) is unchanged, so existing cache entries continue to serve pre-flip results for up to the TTL (DEFAULT_CACHE_TTL = 4 hours). After flipping any such env flag in production, call POST /api/admin/refresh-cache (which invokes revalidateTag on 'dashboard', 'sga-hub', 'sgm-hub') or wait for TTL expiry. Deployment alone does not flush this cache — it's Vercel-account-scoped, not deployment-scoped.

proposed_record.summary: Flipping an env flag that changes runtime query results does NOT invalidate unstable_cache — must call revalidateTag after the flip
proposed_record.decision: When a feature flag is flipped via env var to change the runtime output of a function wrapped in cachedQuery / unstable_cache, the cache key (derived from function name + serialized args) is unchanged, so existing cache entries continue to serve pre-flip results for up to the TTL (DEFAULT_CACHE_TTL = 4 hours). After flipping any such env flag in production, call POST /api/admin/refresh-cache (which invokes revalidateTag on 'dashboard', 'sga-hub', 'sgm-hub') or wait for TTL expiry. Deployment alone does not flush this cache — it's Vercel-account-scoped, not deployment-scoped.
proposed_record.rationale:
Observed 2026-04-21: after ATTRIBUTION_MODEL=v2 was correctly set on Vercel production (clean value, no trailing newline) and the app redeployed, Craig Suchodolski Q2 2025 Contacted→MQL continued showing 25.8% (v1) instead of 3.1% (v2) until cache flush. Next.js 14 App Router unstable_cache is persistent across deploys and keys only on explicit keyName + serialized args — the env branch inside the function body does not affect the cache key.</rationale>
<parameter name="revisit_conditions">If Next.js / Vercel introduces env-aware cache keying, or if the project migrates away from unstable_cache to a different caching layer.

alternatives_considered.approach: Include env flag value in the cache key
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Requires modifying every cachedQuery callsite; cachedQuery wrapper doesn't know which env vars are relevant. Error-prone and easy to miss a callsite.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Not implemented — chose explicit revalidation on flip instead

alternatives_considered.approach: Set a short TTL on flag-gated queries
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Gives up the 4-hour cache benefit for all queries to handle a rare flag flip
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Not implemented

commit_message: chore(schema-context): annotate Contact + Account for ARR-per-advisor

proposed_record.summary: Establish Contact as canonical source for producing-advisor counts in per-advisor metrics.
proposed_record.decision: Per-advisor ARR and other per-advisor metrics must derive advisor counts from SavvyGTMData.Contact filtered by Team_Role__c = 'Advisor' AND Contact_Status__c = 'Joined' AND Churn_Date__c IS NULL. Do NOT use Account.Number_of_Advisors__c (stale roll-up) or row counts from vw_joined_advisors (opportunity grain). Account-level totals (Account_Total_ARR__c, Account_Total_AUM__c) are firm-wide and must be divided by the Contact-derived count to obtain per-advisor metrics.
proposed_record.rationale: The commit explicitly documents a critical schema gap: ARR is stored at firm level on Account, but queries need per-advisor granularity. Account.Number_of_Advisors__c is unreliable (unknown formula, may include churned or non-advisor roles), and vw_joined_advisors is opportunity-grained. Contact is the only table with reliable Team_Role and temporal state filters (Contact_Status, Churn_Date) to distinguish currently-producing advisors from support staff and churned contacts. This is load-bearing for ARR-per-advisor calculations and establishes a durable convention for all downstream per-person metrics.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use Account.Number_of_Advisors__c as the denominator for per-advisor metrics
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Salesforce roll-up of unknown formula; may include onboarding, churned, or non-advisor roles. Stale and unreliable for real-time per-advisor calculations.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Over-count advisors (include churned/onboarding), under-count (exclude support staff), inflate per-advisor ARR

alternatives_considered.approach: Use vw_joined_advisors row counts grouped by account
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: View is opportunity-grain (one row per StageName='Joined' opp), not advisor-grain. One advisor may have multiple opps; one account may have multiple opps. Creates inflated advisor counts and duplicated ARR attribution.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Opportunity-level duplication, many-to-many advisor–opp relationships incorrectly aggregated to advisor counts

alternatives_considered.approach: Create a new denormalized advisor-count rollup table
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Unnecessary complexity and stale-data risk (would need refresh cadence). Contact table already has the required filters and is synced every 6 hours from Salesforce.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Maintenance burden, refresh lag, duplication of Contact state

commit_message: fix(schema-context): re-anchor advisor count on vw_funnel_master, demote Contact

proposed_record.summary: Re-anchor canonical advisor-count authority to vw_funnel_master; demote Contact view.
proposed_record.decision: For all metrics requiring recruiting-verified producing-advisor counts (e.g., arr_per_advisor), anchor on vw_funnel_master (Tableau_Views.vw_funnel_master) with filter is_joined_unique=1 AND recordtypeid='012Dn000000mrO3IAI', not on Contact table. This is the system of record for advisor joins. Contact may only be used as a secondary cross-check for individual-level churn within still-active firms, or for support-staff classification (Team_Role__c IN ('Assistant','CSA')). Document Contact as explicitly NON-authoritative for advisor counts, with evidence of 11-12 row systematic over-count vs. recruiting-funnel authority (125 vs. 113-114).
proposed_record.rationale: Live testing revealed Contact filtered to Team_Role__c='Advisor' AND Contact_Status__c='Joined' AND Churn_Date__c IS NULL returns 125 rows, but the recruiting-funnel authority (vw_funnel_master) shows 113-114 currently-joined advisors. Root cause: team members are added to firms post-join without coming through recruiting opportunities, creating systematic over-count. This commit fixes the immediate metric (arr_per_advisor) and establishes a durable precedent to prevent future analyst errors in all advisor-headcount derivations.

alternatives_considered.approach: Continue using Contact as primary source with manual reconciliation per query
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Does not prevent systematic over-count; places burden on every downstream analyst; vw_funnel_master already exists as system of record.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Future metrics will silently use wrong counts (125 vs. 114) unless author catches it.

alternatives_considered.approach: Blend vw_funnel_master and Contact (funnel rows + Contact churn checks)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Over-complicates the canonical count and duplicates logic already present in vw_funnel_master's is_joined_unique filter. vw_funnel_master already includes firm-level and individual churn logic.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Adds unnecessary joins and increases risk of off-by-one errors in churn logic.

commit_message: fix(vw_close_won): identify advisors by Team_Role with CRD fallback

proposed_record.summary: Advisor identification uses Team_Role primary signal with CRD fallback
proposed_record.decision: Identify individual advisors in vw_close_won by Contact.Team_Role__c = 'Advisor' as the primary filter, falling back to Contact.FA_CRD__c IS NOT NULL only when Team_Role__c is NULL. Explicitly exclude Contacts with Team_Role in ('Assistant','CSA','Other') regardless of CRD status.
proposed_record.rationale: The previous CRD-only filter (FA_CRD__c IS NOT NULL) excluded legitimate advisors whose CRDs had not yet been backfilled in Salesforce, including Brian Boswell and 10+ others (count: 116 → 146). Team_Role__c is now the authoritative source for role classification; CRD serves as a fallback only for legacy records where role data was not yet set. This pattern accommodates both forward-looking (role-primary) and backcompat (CRD-fallback) scenarios while filtering out non-advisor roles that may incidentally carry CRDs.

alternatives_considered.approach: Keep CRD-only filter (FA_CRD__c IS NOT NULL)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Excludes valid advisors whose CRD backfill is in-flight; data coverage gap and inarguably missing advisors from reporting.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Under-counting of active advisors; incomplete pipeline visibility.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use Team_Role__c only, no CRD fallback
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Ignores legacy records where Team_Role has never been populated; would exclude existing backcompat advisors during the data migration window.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Spike in apparent churn as historical records lose their advisor classification; breakage of prior-period continuity.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use Team_Role OR CRD, without explicit exclusion of Assistant/CSA/Other
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Incidental CRDs on non-advisor roles would misclassify paraplanners or support staff as advisors, polluting advisor-level metrics.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Inflated advisor counts; incorrect allocation of revenue/activity to non-advisors.

feat(scorecards): advisor-level Joined/Signed counts with toggles and per-toggle AUM

proposed_record.summary: Scorecard metrics shift from opportunity-grain to advisor-grain with metric-specific toggle dispositions
proposed_record.decision: Joined and Signed scorecard metrics now count individual Contact records (advisors) instead of Opportunities. Each metric has its own disposition enum and toggle state: Joined supports All/Current/Churned; Signed supports All/Joined/Lost. Drill-down routes accept Contact (003) IDs to render advisor-level rows and open individual modals.
proposed_record.rationale: The commit replaces opportunity-centric counting with advisor-centric counting to reflect advisor onboarding and deal-close workflows. New type-safe enums (JoinedDisposition, SignedDisposition) replace the generic MetricDisposition for these two metrics, enabling metric-specific toggle options without cluttering a unified disposition type. Supporting NULL-joined-date advisors in the alltime preset ensures orphaned Contacts are captured; Contact ID routing enables individual activity timelines.

alternatives_considered.approach: Extend a single unified Disposition enum with all possible values (All, Current, Churned, Joined, Lost)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would create a many-to-many relationship between metrics and invalid disposition values, reducing type safety and complicating validation logic in components.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Caller could set Churned on Signed metric or Lost on Joined, requiring runtime validation.

alternatives_considered.approach: Keep opportunity-grain counting with an advisor aggregation layer
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Opportunity counts do not reflect actual advisor lifecycle events (join date, churn) or match the business logic of team expansion and advisor retention tracking.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Metric accuracy degradation; opportunity-to-advisor mapping introduces cardinality mismatches (multiple opps per advisor, orphaned advisors).

commit_message: feat(scorecards): advisor-level Joined/Signed counts with toggles and per-toggle AUM

proposed_record.summary: Switch advisor-grain metrics from opportunity-level to contact-level counts with state toggles.
proposed_record.decision: Joined and Signed scorecard metrics now count individual advisors (Contacts) rather than opportunities. Joined supports toggles: All / Current (live Account_Total_AUM__c) / Churned. Signed supports toggles: All / Joined / Lost. Drill-downs are advisor-grain with per-contact modals; routing accepts Contact (003) IDs to link to individual activity timelines. Cohort logic restricts to actual Joined StageName (excludes future-dated scheduled joins); NULL-joined-date contacts are included in Alltime preset to capture legitimate Joined Contacts without an Opp.
proposed_record.rationale: This commit shifts the unit of analysis from Opportunities (deal count) to Contacts (individual advisor count) to better reflect business reality: scorecards should track people onboarding, not abstract deal stages. The toggle pattern (All/Current/Churned for Joined; All/Joined/Lost for Signed) supports both historical retention analysis and real-time AUM visibility. The inclusion of NULL-joined-date advisors and the explicit StageName filter prevent phantom cohorts (scheduled joins, illegitimate rows) while preserving legitimate edge cases like Michael McCarthy.

alternatives_considered.approach: Keep opportunity-grain metrics, add a Contact join at query time for detail drill-downs
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would require dual aggregate paths (Opp-level summary, Contact-level detail), making it harder to reconcile counts and explain which contacts map to which deals. Single advisor-grain view simplifies UX and analytics logic.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Users see mismatched counts between cards and drilled details, or toggles can't cleanly filter because state is tied to Opportunities, not Contacts.

alternatives_considered.approach: Exclude NULL-joined-date contacts from Alltime preset to keep cohorts strictly StageName-driven
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would lose legitimate Joined Contacts without an Opp (e.g., Michael McCarthy), understating true advisor growth. The commit explicitly includes them to preserve data fidelity.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Undercount of onboarded advisors; unexplained gaps when SFDC data is incomplete or advisors are added outside the standard Joined flow.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use advisor_join_date__c as the sole cohort signal instead of adding StageName filter
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would incorrectly classify future-dated scheduled joins (Wealth Haus) as 'Joined' when they are actually still in_flight. StageName filter ensures the cohort label matches deal reality.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Phantom 'Joined' counts and confusion between 'scheduled to join' and 'actually joined'.

commit_message: docs(schema-context): annotate Lead-attribution pattern + corrected cohort logic

proposed_record.summary: Bridge advisor rows to Lead attribution via ConvertedContactId with per-field team-level fallback
proposed_record.decision: When enriching Contact-grain advisor views with Lead attribution (Source, Channel, SGA, SGM, Lead Score Tier, Campaign), use Lead.ConvertedContactId to join individual advisors to their own converted Lead, then pivot to account-level Lead aggregates (via MAX per field) for advisors without a converted Lead. Apply COALESCE(contact_level, account_level) priority to avoid NULL fields in one Lead masking populated fields from other Leads on the same Account. Document NULL joined_date edge cases (advisors on Joined Accounts with no joining Opportunity) so query authors correctly handle alltime vs. period-specific filtering.
proposed_record.rationale: The commit adds a concrete query pattern to bridge the gap between Contact-grain advisor data (vw_close_won, vw_signed_advisors) and Lead-sourced attribution fields, which are not natively present in those views. The pattern prioritizes advisor-owned Leads while gracefully degrading to team-level aggregates, and documents a known data anomaly (Michael McCarthy) to prevent alltime queries from over-counting NULL-dated advisors into every time period. This becomes a reusable enrichment pattern for analyst tooling.

alternatives_considered.approach: Materialize attribution columns directly into vw_close_won and vw_signed_advisors via LEFT JOIN Lead + vw_funnel_master at view definition time
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Adds persistence and syncing burden; the query pattern documents the intent once so analysts can apply it ad-hoc without modifying base views.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: View churn if Lead attribution schema changes or if per-field MAX logic becomes untenable with a large number of account-level Leads.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use only contact_level Lead attribution, omitting the account_level fallback for advisors without converted Leads
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Leaves advisors without own Leads as completely unenriched, losing attribution context that may be attributed at team/account level.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Incomplete analyst visibility into source/channel for advisors who joined but never had a lead themselves.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use earliest Lead (MIN CreatedDate) for team fallback instead of MAX per field
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Does not address the original problem: one Lead's NULL SGM masking another's populated SGM. Per-field MAX is more robust.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Analyst queries would still see sparse team-level attribution when multiple Leads exist with heterogeneous field population.

commit_message: fix(analyst-bot): match production v2 SGA attribution and disclose date anchors

proposed_record.summary: Per-SGA attribution defaults to primary_sga_name (v2 production mode); system accounts hard-denied; non-canonical date anchors require disclosure.
proposed_record.decision: When generating per-SGA opp-era metrics (SQO volume, Signed, Joined, AUM), default to `primary_sga_name` via LEFT JOIN to vw_lead_primary_sga (v2 production attribution), pair with canonical date anchors (Date_Became_SQO__c for SQO, Stage_Entered_Signed__c for Signed, advisor_join_date__c for Joined), and always exclude system accounts (Savvy Operations, Savvy Marketing, Russell Moss, Jed Entin). When a user specifies a non-canonical date anchor, answer literally but add a mandatory disclosure footer naming the canonical anchor and offering to re-run.
proposed_record.rationale: The bot previously used INNER JOIN on Opp_SGA_Name__c which silently dropped NULL opps, lacked system-account exclusion, and allowed arbitrary date anchors, causing Q4 2025 SQO counts to diverge from production (Amy 10 vs 7, Craig 11 vs 9). This fix aligns the bot with production v2 attribution (primary_sga_name), applies the established system_user_denylist, and enforces canonical date anchors with transparency when users override them. Verified against BigQuery to match dashboard.

alternatives_considered.approach: INNER JOIN on Opp_SGA_Name__c alone (old bot pattern)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Silently undercounts opps where Opp_SGA_Name__c is NULL but SGA_Owner_Name__c contains the true SGA, creating systematic under-attribution.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Produces Q4 2025 Amy≈10, Craig≈11 instead of production Amy=7, Craig=9.

alternatives_considered.approach: 3-tier COALESCE(sga_user.Name, Opp_SGA_Name__c, SGA_Owner_Name__c) for all per-SGA queries
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Correct only for SGA Hub leaderboard context; production dashboards use v2 (primary_sga_name), so applying 3-tier to all queries breaks default parity and creates inconsistent attribution across bot contexts.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Produces Amy=8, Craig=8 for general queries instead of Amy=7, Craig=9, misaligning with most dashboards.

alternatives_considered.approach: Allow arbitrary date anchors without disclosure
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Users may unknowingly request non-canonical anchors (e.g., Opp_CreatedDate instead of Date_Became_SQO__c), producing valid but non-comparable results without awareness of the discrepancy.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Users cannot distinguish between canonical-anchor results and custom-anchor results, leading to silent data misinterpretation.

commit_message: fix(analyst-bot): pin canonical open_sql/open_sqo/open_pipeline definitions

proposed_record.summary: Pin canonical stage definitions for SQL vs SQO vs open pipeline with ban pattern rule
proposed_record.decision:
Define and enforce three distinct stage filters for opportunity funnel analysis:
1. **open_sql**: `is_sql = 1 AND LOWER(COALESCE(SQO_raw,'')) != 'yes' AND (StageName IS NULL OR StageName != 'Closed Lost') AND recordtypeid = '012Dn000000mrO3IAI'` — opp converted from lead but not yet SQO'd.
2. **open_sqo**: `is_sqo_unique = 1 AND is_primary_opp_record = 1 AND StageName NOT IN ('Closed Lost', 'Joined', 'Signed') AND advisor_join_date__c IS NULL AND recordtypeid = '012Dn000000mrO3IAI'` — SQO that hasn't joined or closed.
3. **open_pipeline_active**: `StageName IN ('Qualifying', 'Discovery', 'Sales Process', 'Negotiating') AND recordtypeid = '012Dn000000mrO3IAI'` — active progression stages only.

Enforce via ban_pattern rule that blocks `is_sql = 1 AND StageName NOT IN` anti-pattern, which incorrectly counts all open opps regardless of SQO status.
proposed_record.rationale: The bot was returning 14 records instead of the expected 2–3 due to misuse of `is_sql = 1` (which is true for every converted opp) combined with stage exclusions. The fix adds the critical `SQO_raw != 'yes'` filter to distinguish SQL-stage opps from SQOs in later stages, verified against dashboard truth (3 for SGA Hub, 2 for Funnel Perf). The ban pattern and glossary document prevent future reoccurrence of this costly semantic error.

alternatives_considered.approach: Create a materialized column (e.g., current_stage_category) that pre-computes these buckets
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would add schema maintenance burden and risk staleness; the filter rule is already precise and documented.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Harder to evolve business logic if stage definitions change; more column sprawl.

alternatives_considered.approach: Only add the ban pattern rule without the detailed stage_filter_glossary and key_filters
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: The glossary entry and cross-references to source code are essential for bots and analysts to understand the correct filter and WHY the old one was wrong.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Developers would ignore the error message and either bypass the rule or reintroduce the anti-pattern elsewhere.

proposed_record.summary: Eval-detail uses full-width eval + modal transcript/KB (not a side pane)
proposed_record.decision: The call-intelligence eval-detail page (`/dashboard/call-intelligence/evaluations/[id]`) renders the evaluation panel full-width. Transcript and KB chunks open as **modals** triggered by a "View transcript" button or by citation pill clicks (transcript pills auto-scroll the modal to the cited utterance; KB pills open the chunk modal; combined pills open both). Do not reintroduce a `lg:grid-cols-2` two-pane layout that puts the transcript on the right.
proposed_record.rationale: Step 5b-1-UI initially shipped with a 50/50 two-pane layout (eval left, transcript right). User feedback during browser validation 2026-05-09: the eval panel felt cramped to the left half of the screen, and the right pane forced KB chunks to compete with the transcript for vertical space. Pivoted to full-width eval + modal transcript/KB. The modal transcript matches the original right-pane width (`max-w-3xl`) so reading width is preserved; full-width eval lets long narratives, multi-column strengths/weaknesses, and the audit-toggle two-column comparison breathe properly. Citation pills still drive the transcript jump UX — they just open the modal at the target utterance instead of scrolling a sticky pane.

alternatives_considered.approach: Two-pane (50/50): eval on the left, sticky transcript on the right (the original Step 5b-1-UI plan)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: User found the eval reading experience cramped — long narratives wrap awkwardly at 50% viewport width and the audit-toggle two-column comparison gets squeezed to ~25% per column.

alternatives_considered.approach: Per-citation popover anchored to each pill (no full transcript view)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Loses the ability to read the surrounding context of a cited utterance. Citations only make sense in the flow of the conversation.

proposed_record.revisit_conditions: If the eval-detail page gains a need for persistent side-by-side transcript reading (e.g., real-time coaching while a call is in progress) reconsider. Also revisit if mobile UX feedback shows the modal is harder to dismiss than a stacked single-column was.

proposed_record.summary: Rubric dimension immutability: non-draft statuses lock structure for referential integrity
proposed_record.decision: Non-draft rubrics (active/archived/system) freeze dimension structure—names, order, and level definitions cannot be edited after initial creation. Only the rubric name label remains mutable on all statuses. Drafts permit full dimension editing. This constraint preserves referential integrity because historical evaluations reference dimension identifiers and ordering.
proposed_record.rationale: Rubric dimensions are the controlled-vocabulary identifiers that evaluations scores reference. Once a rubric is activated or archived, changing dimension structure would break the semantic consistency of past evaluation scores and make cross-version comparisons ambiguous. Allowing only name edits on non-drafts gives admins labeling flexibility without corrupting historical data integrity. The commit explicitly decouples `nameLocked` (false always) from `dimensionsLocked` (true for system OR non-draft status).

alternatives_considered.approach: Immutable rubrics after activation (all fields locked)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Prevents legitimate admin corrections to labeling and violates principle of least restriction—name changes carry no referential risk.

alternatives_considered.approach: Allow dimension edits on archived, migrate old evaluation references
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Creates migration burden and temporal inconsistency: old evals would point to redefined dimensions. Violates single-source-of-truth for dimension semantics.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Evaluations referencing renamed or reordered dimensions become ambiguous or orphaned

alternatives_considered.approach: Track dimension ID separately from name; allow renames only
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Out of scope for this commit and adds schema burden; current approach (position + name as identity) is simpler for the governance model already in place.

proposed_record.summary: Single-flight save queue with ref-based OCC for concurrent draft edits
proposed_record.decision: Editor drafts are persisted via a single-flight save queue that uses Refs to track in-flight promises, pending drafts, and server edit_version tokens. On 409 conflict, the client fetches the fresh state and merges it into the editor, then branches on HTTP status code rather than error type for 409-specific handling. Clients do NOT catch and handle CallNoteConflictError; the API bridge maps it to 409 at the route level so the client only needs status-based branching.
proposed_record.rationale: The commit implements a pattern to prevent lost updates when two tabs or actors edit the same note concurrently. By holding state in Refs (editVersionRef, inFlightRef, pendingDraftRef) and draining the queue only when prior requests complete, it guarantees at most one save-flight at a time while queuing the most recent draft. Defense-in-depth 409 mapping at the route layer decouples the client from the API's CallNoteConflictError class, reducing coupling.

alternatives_considered.approach: Pessimistic locking (lock note on open, release on close)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Breaks UX when reps work in parallel tabs; adds session state burden on backend and complicates timeout/cleanup.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Multiple reps unable to review the same note in separate tabs.

alternatives_considered.approach: Client-side conflict retries using exponential backoff with full error class propagation
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Adds complexity to every client component that calls the note-review API; mixes transport concerns (409) with domain logic (CallNoteConflictError).
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Tight coupling between client and API error types; harder to reuse across clients (web vs mobile).

alternatives_considered.approach: Multi-flight concurrent saves with last-write-wins merge
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Risk of lost updates if one flight succeeds after another; race conditions on edit_version updates.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: User A edits, User B edits and saves, User A's save arrives late and overwrites User B's version.

commit_message: feat(call-intelligence): SuggestedRecordsPanel — waterfall candidates in the app

proposed_record.summary: Optional schema fields enable safe backwards-compatible cross-service feature rollouts.
proposed_record.decision: When rolling out new fields in shared response schemas that are not yet available in all upstream service versions, make the field optional and nullable in the consumer schema. Absent fields parse as undefined, allowing the consumer to gracefully fall back to prior behavior until the upstream is fully deployed. Do not gate on version checks or feature flags; rely on schema optionality.
proposed_record.rationale: This commit demonstrates backwards-compatible cross-service rollout via optional sfdc_suggestion: old Cloud Run revisions return without the field, the Dashboard schema parses it as undefined, and the UI falls through to the existing search-only interaction. This pattern avoids coordination barriers and deployment order constraints, allowing feature velocity while maintaining safety across service boundaries.

alternatives_considered.approach: Add a version check or feature flag in the consumer to gate on upstream readiness
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Requires explicit coordination and flag management; optional schema fields achieve the same safety with zero operational overhead.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Feature flag flip could be missed during rollout; version checks add coupling logic to the consumer.

alternatives_considered.approach: Mandate all upstream services deploy together before rolling out in consumers
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Creates deployment order dependencies and slows feature velocity; optional fields eliminate this bottleneck.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Serialization of deployments across service boundaries increases time-to-value and complexity.

Admin/revops_admin bypass rep existence checks but still validate visibility. Non-privileged actors without a coaching-reps row receive notFound() if focus_rep is set, preventing indirect rep discovery via 403/404 response patterns.
proposed_record.rationale: The Insights modal stack feature adds deep-linking capability via focus_rep query param. To prevent unauthorized rep existence disclosure, visibility checks must happen server-side and return notFound() rather than 403, which would leak that a rep exists. The commit gates access through getRepIdByEmail + getRepIdsVisibleToActor, with a fast-path exception for privileged roles that have unrestricted rep scope anyway.

alternatives_considered.approach: Client-side redirect on visibility check failure
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Leaks rep existence: client-side code would require fetching rep details before auth check, exposing the rep in the response.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Unauthorized actors learn which rep IDs exist in the system.

alternatives_considered.approach: Return 403 Forbidden for visibility violations
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Still leaks rep existence implicitly; 403 signals the resource exists but is forbidden, whereas 404 is ambiguous.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Attackers enumerate valid rep IDs by probing focus_rep param.

commit_message: URL-keyed fetch effects must depend on searchParams.toString(), not derived useMemo values
diff_summary: Any `useEffect` that triggers a data fetch and reads filter state from Next.js 14's `useSearchParams()` MUST key on `searchParams.toString()` (a stable string), not on the `useSearchParams()` reference itself nor on derived `useMemo([searchParams])` values nor on a `useCallback` wrapping those derived values. In components that combine a `window.history.pushState` modal/sidebar stack with URL-derived data fetches, this is the load-bearing pattern that prevents spurious refires on hash-only URL changes.

proposed_record.summary: URL-keyed fetch effects must depend on searchParams.toString(), not derived useMemo values
proposed_record.decision: Any `useEffect` that triggers a data fetch and reads filter state from Next.js 14's `useSearchParams()` MUST key on `searchParams.toString()` (a stable string), not on the `useSearchParams()` reference itself nor on derived `useMemo([searchParams])` values nor on a `useCallback` wrapping those derived values. In components that combine a `window.history.pushState` modal/sidebar stack with URL-derived data fetches, this is the load-bearing pattern that prevents spurious refires on hash-only URL changes.
proposed_record.rationale: In Next.js 14 App Router, `useSearchParams()` returns a new `ReadonlyURLSearchParams` reference whenever the URL changes for ANY reason — including hash-only updates from `window.history.pushState`. In `InsightsTab.tsx`, the modal-stack hash sync (`pushState('...#modal=detail&eval=...')` on each layer push) was invalidating the `searchParams` reference, which cascaded through every `useMemo([searchParams])` (dateRange, role, podIds, repIds, sourceFilter, trendMode), then through the `useCallback` wrapping `fetchData`, then the `useEffect([fetchData])` re-fired all four page-level APIs (heatmap, clusters, pods, reps) on every modal layer transition. The `setLoading(true)` flip blanked the heat map / cluster list for ~200ms, visible as a "flash" behind the modal stack. Confirmed 2026-05-12 via server-log instrumentation: on a Layer 1 → Layer 2 row click, `/insights/heatmap`, `/insights/clusters`, `/insights/pods`, `/insights/reps` all fired even though no filter changed. Switching the effect dep to `searchParams.toString()` eliminated the refire because the string is unchanged when only the hash mutates.

alternatives_considered.approach: Memoize the heat-map and cluster-list JSX with useMemo to avoid re-render even when fetchData fires
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Doesn't solve the root cause — fetchData would still refire setLoading(true), still hide content. Treats symptom not cause.

alternatives_considered.approach: Remove window.history.pushState hash sync entirely so the URL doesn't change on modal transitions
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Loses the browser-back-pops-layer affordance the modal stack relies on, and doesn't help any future component that combines URL hash with searchParams-derived data.

alternatives_considered.approach: Wrap fetchData in useCallback with empty deps + read state via refs
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Defers the stale-closure problem to every state read inside fetchData. Adds ref boilerplate for every filter value. Less robust than keying on the URL string proxy.

proposed_record.revisit_conditions: If Next.js changes useSearchParams behavior in a future major (e.g. stable reference across hash changes), this can be relaxed. If the modal-stack hash sync is removed entirely, the immediate symptom disappears but the rule still applies to any future URL-state component.

proposed_record.summary: Enforce pre-query schema consultation via hard-gate rule for all Neon SQL writes
proposed_record.decision: Before writing or modifying any SQL against the Neon sales_coaching database, contributors must (a) call mcp__Neon__describe_table_schema for the live column list, and (b) consult the relevant table section in .claude/neon-savvy-dashboard.md or .claude/neon-sales-coaching.md for business purpose, grain, and known traps. This is a HARD GATE enforced at code review and documented prominently in CLAUDE.md.
proposed_record.rationale: The commit introduces curated, high-fidelity schema documentation (.claude/neon-sales-coaching.md with 597 lines covering 43 tables, 44-column hot tables, JSONB shapes, and schema drift risks) alongside a hard-gate rule to prevent silent runtime failures. The documentation highlights critical traps (e.g., JSONB column shapes like evaluations.dimension_scores, the direct pg.Pool dependency in Dashboard, and a two-tier read/write split) that make schema consultation non-negotiable. This is a load-bearing architectural safeguard for a high-cardinality, direct-access database connection.

alternatives_considered.approach: Rely on auto-generated schema introspection queries only; no requirement to consult curated docs
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Live introspection does not surface business semantics, grain mismatches, or footgun patterns (e.g., the shape of nested JSONB columns or the two-tier read/write split). The commit message explicitly rejects this as insufficient.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Silent query bugs when column semantics are misunderstood or when JSONB shapes diverge from assumptions.

alternatives_considered.approach: Document schema in a single README at the repo root, no explicit gate
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Soft documentation is easy to overlook. The commit elevates this to a hard gate in CLAUDE.md (an AI system prompt context) to make it binding for all future SQL authoring, not optional.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Contributors skip or misremember documentation, leading to schema drift bugs.

proposed_record.summary: Curated schema docs + document-neon-schema skill as runtime gate for Neon SQL
proposed_record.decision: All Claude sessions writing SQL against Neon Postgres databases (savvy-dashboard and sales-coaching) must first consult the curated schema context documents at `.claude/neon-<db-name>.md` and execute the `/document-neon-schema` skill. These documents capture business purpose, grain, JSONB shapes, consumer sites, and intent traps — information that raw column introspection cannot provide. The skill spawns parallel agents (schema-introspector, consumer-mapper, jsonb-shape-sampler) to keep schema docs fresh and actionable. This is enforced via a hard gate in CLAUDE.md.
proposed_record.rationale: The commit introduces curated schema documentation as a runtime guard against SQL anti-patterns and schema misunderstanding. Rather than relying on raw Neon MCP introspection alone, the skill captures business context (table purpose, dormancy status, dangerous columns), consumer mapping (which API routes and server actions touch each table), and JSONB shapes — the "what the schema cannot tell you" layer. By making this a hard-gate prerequisite, the codebase ensures that every SQL writer has both live schema facts and business context before executing queries.

alternatives_considered.approach: Rely solely on raw Neon MCP introspection (describe_table_schema, run_sql)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Does not capture business purpose, grain, consumer sites, dormancy flags, or JSONB shape nuances — leaves writers vulnerable to selecting from dead tables, writing to high-cardinality columns, or misunderstanding denormalization choices.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Incorrect SQL assumptions, unnecessary table joins, writes to wrong grain, JSONB shape mismatches.

alternatives_considered.approach: Maintain schema docs manually without automated refresh skill
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Docs drift quickly as schema evolves; manual updates are error-prone and create stale information liability.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Docs fall out of sync with live schema; reviewers cannot trust doc warnings; obsolete column references.

commit_message: feat(call-intel): add Needs Linking sub-tab to Coaching Usage

proposed_record.summary: Sub-tab orchestration pattern within call-intelligence tabs via wrapper component.
proposed_record.decision: CoachingUsageWrapper delegates role-aware sub-tab state management and visibility to a top-level client component that renders mutually exclusive sub-views (overview and needs-linking). Sub-tabs are UI-driven (not routed), with role determining default tab and visibility of the overview option (revops_admin only). Sub-tab changes refetch data via callback.
proposed_record.rationale: The commit introduces a multi-level tab hierarchy to call-intelligence: a parent "Coaching Usage" tab now hosts child "overview" and "needs-linking" sub-tabs, with role-based access control. CoachingUsageWrapper centralizes this orchestration as a lightweight client component, avoiding route complexity while allowing SGM/manager roles to default to needs-linking view. This pattern is reusable for future call-intel sub-tab additions.

alternatives_considered.approach: Route sub-tabs as distinct pages (e.g., /call-intelligence/coaching-usage/overview and /coaching-usage/needs-linking)
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Adds unnecessary routing complexity and URL depth for what are UI toggles within a single logical tab.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Would clutter next.js routing, complicate back-button navigation, and make refresh behavior inconsistent with sibling tabs.

alternatives_considered.approach: Flatten both views into a single CoachingUsageTab with conditional renders and filters
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Collapses UX separation and makes role-based default behavior implicit in component logic rather than explicit.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Would violate principle of least surprise when managers see different defaults; harder to add additional sub-tabs later.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use context or prop-drilling to pass sub-tab state from parent page component down through multiple levels
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Wrapper component encapsulates sub-tab logic cleanly; context would over-engineer a localized UI concern.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Adds unnecessary provider boilerplate and makes sub-tab state global when it is feature-local.

commit_message: fix(schema-context): replace invalid disclosure_required rule type with prefer_field

proposed_record.summary: Correct invalid rule type in schema validation config
proposed_record.decision: The canonical_stage_volume_date_anchor rule must use type "prefer_field" instead of the invalid "disclosure_required" type. This rule enforces preference for canonical date anchors (Opp_CreatedDate, Date_Became_SQO__c, advisor_join_date__c) in stage-volume queries while allowing user-specified anchors with disclosure context.
proposed_record.rationale: The "disclosure_required" type was not in the valid rule type enumeration (ban_pattern, prefer_field, require_filter, date_type_rule), causing schema config validation failures in the MCP health check. "prefer_field" better captures the intended behavior: recommending canonical date anchors while tolerating user-specified alternatives with disclosure context.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use ban_pattern to strictly prohibit non-canonical date anchors
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Too restrictive; the commit message explicitly requires supporting user-specified anchors with disclosure, not outright bans.
alternatives_considered.failure_conditions: Would prevent legitimate user requests for alternative date anchors.

alternatives_considered.approach: Use require_filter to mandate canonical anchors in queries
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Semantically misaligned; require_filter enforces filtering constraints, not field preference recommendations.

alternatives_considered.approach: Leave disclosure_required and extend the valid rule type enum
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Would require schema changes across the validation engine; the rule semantics map directly to existing prefer_field capability.
